children.title: Update memory_search tool to use current retrieval pipeline
children.description: Unify auto-injection (index.js) to use retrieval.js pipeline. Currently index.js has its own multi-layer search + FTS path while retrieval.js has knowledge graph + chunk replacement + neural reranking. Auto-injection should call retrieveMemories() from retrieval.js instead of reimplementing search. This ensures production injection matches LoCoMo benchmark performance (0.927 composite). Key features to gain: chunk replacement, graph traversal with centrality, lazy query expansion.

notes.text: CHECKPOINT: CC dawn-cedar completed. Benchmark post-OR-removal: composite 0.927 (-0.001 vs baseline). Recall@5=1.0 across all categories. LLM judge adversarial 2.2/5, temporal 3.7/5 are key quality gaps. memory_search tool update still pending.

CRITICAL for #243 merge: chunks have zero/null vectors by design — they are never embedded independently. They are linked to their parent refined memory via chunk_group_id. The multi-layer chunk search in index.js does vector search on refined memories only, then retrieves linked chunks. When merging retrieval.js chunk-replacement logic, do NOT try to embed chunks or do vector search on them. The correct flow is: vector search refined memories → look up linked chunks → optionally replace refined with chunks for richer context. Also: production DB currently has zero chunk records (only refined types: user_stated, agent_conclusion, session_summary, system_event, external). FTS in index.js is production-only and valuable — preserve it in the merge.

Production DB has zero chunks currently — extraction pipeline only writes refined memories. Need to also store source conversation chunks (with zero vectors) during extraction and link via chunk_group_id. This is prerequisite for chunk-replacement in retrieval. The bench already has this working via pre-extracted fixtures. Part of the unification work or may belong in #244 (re-extraction).
memory_search tool knowledgeGraph config passthrough fixed in tools.js. Verified in benchmark run.

Production must store chunks during extraction and do chunk replacement at retrieval time. This is what closes the 20pp gap. Bench proves it works. Production index.js currently serves refined memories only.

This ensures all memories use the latest extraction model, chunking, entity resolution, and importance scoring. Prerequisite: #243 (unified retrieval) complete + LoCoMo score stable.

children.title: Add chunk window size config to openclaw.json
children.description: Expose chunkConversation windowSize as a configurable setting in openclaw.json (e.g. memoryhub.chunking.windowSize). Currently hardcoded as DEFAULT_WINDOW_SIZE=3 in src/chunk.js. Default stays 3. Also add topicAware toggle. Enables experimentation without code changes.

children.title: Embedding-based entity deduplication for knowledge graph
children.description: Current entity resolution uses Jaro-Winkler string similarity only. Misses semantic matches (grandmother vs grandma). New flow: (1) Haiku suggests entity with description, (2) embed suggestion and search existing entities in graph, (3) present top-N matches with IDs to Haiku, (4) Haiku decides merge vs create new. This closes the entity fragmentation gap where the same real-world entity gets multiple graph nodes.

notes.text: Architecture decision (2026-03-23): Entity extraction should be part of memory extraction, not a separate pass. Two-step flow: (1) Haiku extracts memories + proposes entities from chunk. (2) Vector similarity search on entity name+description against existing entity DB → Haiku picks existing entity or creates new one with UUID. This gives online dedup at extraction time. Entities get embeddings on name+description for the similarity search. Replaces current post-hoc resolver approach. Part of #247 unified pipeline scope.

Graph design decision (2026-03-23): Every entity-entity relation is a separate edge tagged with chunk/memory ID. No edge merging, no weight incrementing. 'talked_to' appears N times rather than 'friend_of' once. Benefits: temporal traversal, evidence trail, frequency-as-signal, no information loss from abstracted labels. Schema: drop weight column, add required chunk_id, always INSERT (never upsert). Edge count between two entities replaces weight. Relation types should be specific observed interactions (talked_to, painted_with, discussed_adoption) not inferred abstractions (friend_of).

children.title: Improve LoCoMo bench execution: bite-size, parallel, batch API, quick functional tests
children.description: Current LoCoMo bench is slow (2-3h for full 10-conv run) and fragile. Improvements: 1) Bite-size: single fake/short conversation for quick functional tests (<2 min). 2) Parallel/async extraction with rate limit awareness (semaphore, respect RPM/TPM). 3) Investigate Anthropic Batch API for extraction (50% cost reduction). 4) --smoke flag: 1 fake conv + 5 queries, validates full pipeline end-to-end. 5) Adaptive concurrency based on 429 responses.

notes.text: Scope expanded: unify bench and production into single ingest pipeline (pipeline.js). Bench reimplements ~400 lines of orchestration that should be shared code. One ingest(messages, agentId, config) function: chunk → extract → resolveEntities → embed → store → buildGraph. Production calls per-turn, bench calls per-conv. Same code path = same bugs = same fixes. Also build graph during extraction (not after) to overlap wait times. Per Andreas directive 2026-03-23.

Architecture decision (2026-03-23): Graph building must happen during extraction, not as separate pass. Two-step entity extraction (see #246 note) feeds directly into graph edges. Centrality scores available immediately after extraction for use in confidence gate (new task). Also: centrality should be part of the composite confidence score for the retrieval confidence gate — not just similarity.

Perf improvement decisions (2026-03-23): (1) Batch entity extraction — send 5-10 memories per Haiku call instead of 1. Returns JSON array. Reduces LLM calls 5-10x. (2) Bench runs require quiet gateway — OAuth token contention causes 401s when gateway agents respond concurrently. Bench is an offline task; acceptable to pause gateway or run off-hours. Do NOT engineer around this (option 3 dedicated API key is over-engineering for a bench tool). These are part of the unified pipeline refactor scope.

children.title: LongMemEval benchmark for MemoryHub
children.description: Implement LongMemEval benchmark alongside LoCoMo. LongMemEval tests cross-session recall, temporal reasoning over longer horizons, and multi-turn dialogue memory — areas LoCoMo doesn't cover (LoCoMo is per-conversation only). Covers: dataset acquisition, adapter implementation, evaluation harness, comparison with LoCoMo results.

children.title: Retrieval confidence gate — filter low-confidence memories before injection
children.description: Add confidence threshold to retrieval pipeline. If ALL top-K results fall below a composite confidence score (α*similarity + β*centrality), return empty array instead of weak memories. Benefits: (1) benchmark — UNANSWERABLE correctly scored when no confident matches, (2) production — less pollution in proactive memory injection. Threshold must be configurable per-agent. Composite score incorporates both vector similarity and graph centrality so graph-connected memories survive even at moderate similarity.

children.title: Experiment: chunk-only retrieval — benchmark without refined memories
children.description: Hypothesis: refined memories may be unnecessary if chunks are embedded directly. Refined memories currently serve as a search index (good embeddings + metadata) but get replaced by source chunks before the judge/agent sees them. Test: (1) Embed chunks with real vectors instead of zero vectors. (2) Run bench with chunk embeddings only — no refined memories at all. (3) Compare R@5 and judge accuracy against current approach (refined memories as search surface + chunk replacement). If chunk embeddings match or beat refined memory embeddings for retrieval precision, drop the refinement step entirely — saves ~50% Haiku extraction cost. If worse, refined memories stay as search index entries (not content). Related: #249 (confidence gate), #246 (entity extraction), #247 (unified pipeline).

notes.text: Current best: R@5=0.8366 (sliding_window w=5). Target: 0.90. Gap: 0.063.
NEW BEST: R@5=0.8983 (entity_agg + sliding_window w=10). Gap to 0.99: 0.092. By cat: sh=0.813, mh=0.876, temporal=0.982, adversarial=0.912, open_domain=0.886.

Phase 4 done. Haiku reranker: R@5=0.9368 (full bench). Ceiling R@50=0.9863 (27/1977 hard misses). By cat: sh=0.900, mh=0.888, temporal=0.996, adv=0.945, od=0.919. Gap to 0.99: 0.053. Next: wider reranker candidates + multi-hop decomposition.

Current best R@5=0.9444 (Phase 5 H1, n=1977). CoT reranker smoke: 0.9847 on conv-26. BLOCKED: both Anthropic API + OpenRouter out of credits. Ceiling R@50=0.9863 < target 0.99 — need credit top-up + embedding fix for 27 hard misses.
Cycle 3 (15:40 CET): R@5=0.9454 (Phase 5 H3, cat-aware reranker). CC running Phase 6: h6_mh_candidates100 + h6_multihop_decompose. Gap: 0.0446. Ceiling: 0.9863.
Phase 6 launched — current best R@5=0.9449 (n=1977). CC running (PID 640561): testing candidates=100 full bench + H_split_naive (conjunction split). Gap to target: 0.0451. Ceiling R@50=0.9863.

Cron cycle 21:01 CET. Best R@5=0.9454 (Phase 5 H3). Launched H6 full bench: reranker_candidates=100+cat-aware. Smoke test: 0.9898 on conv-26 alone. CC running PID 674162. Ceiling R@50=0.9863 — 27 hard misses may keep final score below 0.99.

notes.text: Phase 2 complete. Best: sliding_window w=5, R@5=0.8366. Key findings: window size dominant lever, BM25+prefix both hurt. Gap to 0.90 = 0.063. CC continuing with w=7, w=10 + Phase 3 query optimization.

Phase 2 done (best w=10, R@5=0.8796). Phase 3 results: dual_index=0.841 (killed), top_k_200=0.8715 (no gain), query_expand=0.862 (adds noise). All retrieval-side approaches exhausted. Moving to ingestion-side: H_entity_agg (Haiku entity summaries).

ideas.title: Evaluate Paperclip for MacroHard orchestration + Clipmart distribution
ideas.description: Paperclip (github.com/paperclipai/paperclip) is open-source orchestration for zero-human companies. Node.js + React UI. 33k stars, MIT. Sits above OpenClaw — manages org charts, budgets, governance, goal alignment across agent teams. Their tagline: 'If OpenClaw is an employee, Paperclip is the company.' Coming soon: Clipmart — one-click company templates marketplace.
ideas.rationale: Two angles: (1) Governance/cost dashboard — we lack unified cost tracking and budget governance across agents. Paperclip may fill that gap better than extending Telos. (2) Clipmart distribution — once MacroHard is proven, publishing as a template ('AI-native freight SaaS company') could be meaningful early distribution. First-mover advantage if Clipmart gains traction.
ideas.key_decisions: 1. Does Paperclip's governance layer outperform Telos, or does it duplicate it? 2. Is Clipmart worth targeting as a distribution channel? 3. Conductor approval needed before any installation. 4. Evaluate after #37 (Pyfio UG) clears — no bandwidth to evaluate infra changes while formation is pending.

ideas.title: Evaluate gstack for MacroHard workflow
ideas.description: gstack (github.com/garrytan/gstack) is Garry Tan's (YC) Claude Code setup: 20+ slash command 'specialists' (CEO review, eng manager, designer, QA, security officer, release engineer). MIT license. 49k+ stars. Turns Claude Code into a structured virtual team. Key commands: /plan-ceo-review, /review, /qa, /ship, /retro, /cso (security), /browse. Install: git clone into ~/.claude/skills/gstack. Could enhance Forge's coding quality, review rigor, and QA coverage.
ideas.rationale: We're a 1-person shop shipping with AI agents. gstack's structured roles (especially /review, /qa, /cso) could catch what Forge currently misses. Karpathy-validated workflow. Low cost (MIT, no dependencies beyond Claude Code).
ideas.key_decisions: 1. Does it add enough value over our current Forge workflow? 2. Which commands map to our pain points (code review, QA, security)? 3. Conductor approval needed before any installation (per skill install policy).

ideas.title: VRE — Volute Reasoning Engine: Epistemic enforcement for agentic AI
ideas.description: GitHub: https://github.com/anormang1992/vre — VRE is a Python library that gives autonomous agents an explicit, inspectable model of what they know before they act. It maintains an epistemic graph of 'primitives' (concepts like file, permission, directory) grounded across depth levels (D0=existence → D4+=implications). Before an agent executes an action, it must demonstrate that the relevant concepts are grounded at the required depth. If not, the action is blocked and the knowledge gap is surfaced explicitly. Uses Neo4j for the knowledge graph. Stack: Python, LangChain/LangGraph, Anthropic/Claude, Neo4j. Potentially related to ToolSense (milestone #182) — ToolSense is about memory-augmented tool execution; VRE adds structural epistemic enforcement (not just memory, but verified understanding) as a prerequisite to tool execution.
ideas.rationale: Addresses a real failure mode: agents acting confidently on knowledge they cannot justify (hallucination, unsafe execution). The epistemic depth model (D0-D4+) could complement ToolSense by adding a verifiable knowledge gate before tool calls. Could also inform MemoryHub's entity enrichment layer.
ideas.key_decisions: Is this a layer on top of ToolSense or a replacement for part of it? Can the epistemic graph be built from MemoryHub's existing entity/relationship data? Is Neo4j the right backend or can we adapt to our stack? How does this interact with human-in-the-loop approval flows?

ideas.title: TelosHub v2: Task-Aware Signal Extraction
ideas.description: Current TelosHub plugin (~/code/macrohard/teloshub/) fires on session_end, sends transcript to Haiku, extracts task signals. Currently 0/8 hit rate — Haiku has no task list so guesses titles instead of matching IDs; only reads assistant messages (misses user directives); no completion signal type; 8k char transcript cap truncates long sessions. Fix: inject open task list into extraction prompt, include both roles, add status_complete, raise cap. Plugin: ~/code/macrohard/teloshub/src/extract.js. Config: openclaw.json plugins.entries.teloshub.
ideas.rationale: Agents reference Telos tasks constantly but manual CLI calls get skipped. Auto-capturing signals would keep Telos current at ~$0.01/session.
ideas.key_decisions: Inject task list per-call or cache? Confidence threshold after ID matching? Auto-complete tasks or just notes? Include user+assistant or assistant-only?

ideas.title: MemoryHub: multi-pass query window + FTS hybrid retrieval
ideas.description: Replace single 3+3 rolling window with multi-pass retrieval: Pass 1 (last 1 msg) for narrow/specific hits, Pass 2 (last 2+2) for relational context, Pass 3 (last 3+3) for thematic. Union all candidate sets, dedup by memory ID, re-rank by best score across passes. Add FTS (BM25/keyword) pass on last 1 user message only — catches exact IDs, names, Telos numbers that embeddings dilute. FTS hits get fixed similarity score (e.g. 0.6) to compete with vector results. Final ranking via RRF (Reciprocal Rank Fusion) combining vector + keyword scores.
ideas.rationale: Current 3+3 window dilutes specific identifiers (#136, 'Matilda', 'Nilo') into thematic embeddings. Multi-pass + FTS directly fixes the observed failure mode where exact references miss the similarity threshold. RRF is proven (MemOS uses it).
ideas.key_decisions: How many candidates per pass before dedup? LanceDB supports FTS index (lance_full_text_search) — add to schema or use JS-side keyword filter as fallback? RRF k parameter tuning? Does multi-pass increase latency noticeably (3 parallel searches vs 1)?

ideas.title: MemoryHub: inject most recent session_summary at session-start
ideas.description: At session initialization, always inject the session_summary of the most recent prior session for that agent as the first memory. This gives the agent immediate continuity — 'last time we spoke about X' — without relying on similarity scoring or recency weighting. Implementation: in the session-start injection path, run a separate query: SELECT * WHERE memory_type='session_summary' AND agent_id=agentId ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 1. Inject this unconditionally before the top-N scored memories. Could also inject the most recent session_summary per channel/conversation if sessions are channel-scoped.
ideas.rationale: Most natural form of continuity. Humans recap the last conversation before starting a new one. Session summaries are purpose-built for this — concise, agent-scoped, high signal. Currently session-start injection uses recency+importance scoring which surfaces operational noise instead.
ideas.key_decisions: One summary or last N (e.g. 3)? Should it count against the topN budget or be additive? Per-agent or per-conversation (channel-scoped)? Should it be shown via showInMessage or silent?

ideas.title: MemoryHub: extract memories from Workflowy
ideas.description: Sync Workflowy outline into memory DB. Parse bullet nodes as facts/preferences/commitments based on content. Workflowy has an OPML export and unofficial API. Could run as periodic sync — detect changed nodes, re-extract.
ideas.rationale: Workflowy is likely a curated knowledge store — high-quality signal. Structured outlines map well to the typed memory model (decisions, facts, commitments).
ideas.key_decisions: Full re-sync or delta? Map Workflowy tags/mirrors to memory_type? How to handle deletions in Workflowy — delete from DB?

ideas.title: MemoryHub: extract memories from YouTube watch history / transcripts
ideas.description: For YouTube videos watched, fetch transcript via youtube-transcript API, run through summarization + extraction. Could hook into browser history or YouTube Takeout watch history. Extracts: topics of interest, learned facts, preferences (channels, subjects).
ideas.rationale: Reveals interests and knowledge consumed passively. High-value for preference modeling.
ideas.key_decisions: Trigger: on-demand (/remember this video) vs automatic from watch history? Transcript quality varies. How to rate-limit (don't extract every cat video)?

ideas.title: MemoryHub: calendar sync — store events as memories with context
ideas.description: Sync calendar events into memory DB as commitment/entity memories. Upcoming events → commitments. Past events → facts (what happened, with whom). Could extract recurring patterns (weekly tennis, doctor appointments). Bidirectional: actionable memories could create calendar events.
ideas.rationale: Calendar is ground truth for Andreas's schedule and social graph. Surfacing upcoming events in memory injection is more natural than raw calendar queries.
ideas.key_decisions: Which calendars? How far ahead to sync? How to handle recurring events (one memory or per-instance)? Privacy of sensitive events?

ideas.title: MemoryHub: actionable memories — extract and surface action items from memories
ideas.description: When extracting memories, identify if content implies a future action ('I'm going to the grocery store' → 'Buy milk next time Andreas goes to grocery store'). Store as memory_type='action' with trigger conditions. On injection, if action memory matches context (e.g. location, topic), surface it prominently. Could integrate with Telos task creation for high-priority actions.
ideas.rationale: Closes the loop between passive memory and active assistance. Transforms MemoryHub from a recall system into a proactive assistant layer.
ideas.key_decisions: How to model trigger conditions (time-based, location-based, topic-based, contact-based)? Separate action_item memory type vs tag on existing types? When does an action expire/complete? Auto-create Telos task or just surface in injection?

ideas.title: MemoryHub: extract memories from WhatsApp, Gmail, Telegram conversations
ideas.description: Run extraction pipeline on external conversation sources: WhatsApp (via wacli), Gmail threads, Telegram history. Parse message threads into conversation format, feed through extractMemoriesInWindows(). WhatsApp already partially covered via relay sessions. Gmail needs agentmail or OAuth. Telegram history exportable via API.
ideas.rationale: Massive signal source currently untapped. Personal conversations contain preferences, commitments, entity facts, decisions that never flow through agent sessions.
ideas.key_decisions: Incremental sync vs one-shot backfill? How to handle privacy (filter contacts)? Dedup against existing memories? Which contacts to include?

ideas.title: MemoryHub: extract memories from ChatGPT/Gemini session exports
ideas.description: Import conversation history exports from ChatGPT (conversations.json) and Gemini. Parse into role/content format, run through extractMemoriesInWindows(). ChatGPT exports available via data export feature. Gemini via Takeout.
ideas.rationale: Years of prior context, decisions, preferences, and knowledge locked in other AI sessions. One-time import would massively bootstrap the memory DB.
ideas.key_decisions: How to attribute agent_id? Separate 'chatgpt-import' agent or merge into main? How to handle stale/outdated facts?

ideas.title: /forget command: exclude recent content from memory extraction
ideas.description: Two variants: (1) /forget_session — marks the entire current session as excluded from extraction at session_end. (2) /forget 5min — excludes everything said in the last N minutes from extraction. Implementation: write a 'forget marker' to session state (e.g. forget_until: timestamp or forget_session: true). The session_end extraction hook checks these markers before calling extractMemories — skips the session or filters out messages within the forget window. Could also retroactively delete already-extracted memories from a session if the command fires mid-session.
ideas.rationale: Privacy control. User may share sensitive info (addresses, personal details, embarrassing context) mid-conversation and want it excluded from long-term memory. Natural UX — mirrors how humans say 'forget I said that'.
ideas.key_decisions: Retroactive vs prospective only? Should /forget 5min also delete already-written memories from that window (requires searching by source_session + timestamp)? Should there be a /forget_all to wipe all memories from current session? How to surface feedback — confirm message 'OK, last 5 min will not be remembered'?

ideas.title: Single-pass backfill: compose summary from tumbling window mini-summaries
ideas.description: Current backfill makes 2 separate passes: one for session_summary (summarizeSession), one for granular extraction (extractMemoriesInWindows). Should be one pass. Each tumbling window already processes a chunk — each window should also produce a mini-summary. Final session_summary is composed by combining mini-summaries. Eliminates redundant API calls and double-reading the conversation.
ideas.rationale: Cuts Haiku API calls roughly in half for large sessions. Cleaner architecture — summary is derived from the same windows as granular extraction, so they stay in sync. Better quality: summary built bottom-up from window insights rather than top-down from raw conversation.
ideas.key_decisions: How to combine mini-summaries: concatenate + final Haiku pass to merge, or just join? Should mini-summaries be stored separately or only the composed final? Does this change the live extraction pipeline too (onSessionEnd) or only backfill?

ideas.title: Graph DB for memory: is it actually better than vector search?
ideas.description: Andreas is skeptical that graph DBs (like in Mem0) provide real retrieval quality improvements over vector search. Relationships between entities are implicitly encoded in embedding space anyway. Our family facts retrieval problems were caused by a bug (wrong distance metric), not by absence of a graph. The question is whether there's actual empirical research showing graph+vector beats vector-only for personal assistant memory retrieval, or if it's architecture intuition that hasn't been validated.
ideas.rationale: Before investing in graph layer for MemoryHub, should verify the improvement is real. Could be over-engineering.
ideas.key_decisions: Is there peer-reviewed research comparing graph+vector vs vector-only for personal memory retrieval? What's the actual retrieval quality delta? Is the complexity cost justified?

ideas.title: MemoryHub — shared global memory network for AI agents
ideas.description: A global memory layer that any agent can tap into. All agent memory lives there and is refined over time. Network effect: the more agents participate, the better it gets. Like Facebook but for agent memories. Privacy layer built in to prevent leaking non-public/personal info. Agents contribute anonymized/sanitized memories and benefit from the collective knowledge of all participating agents.
ideas.rationale: Current agent memory is siloed per-agent, per-deployment. A shared substrate would eliminate cold-start problems, enable cross-deployment learning, and create a defensible network-effect moat. MacroHard is already building memory infrastructure — this is a natural productization path.
ideas.key_decisions: Privacy architecture: how to strip PII before contributing? Opt-in vs opt-out contribution model? Federated vs centralized? Monetization: API access fees, premium tiers? How to prevent poisoning/manipulation of shared memory? What's the trust model for contributed memories?

ideas.title: Telos DB health check: alert on node count divergence
ideas.description: If telos.db node count vs API node count diverges by >10, alert Andreas. Root cause: DB can be deleted/replaced on disk while API process holds it open via fd — silent divergence. Happened 2026-03-03 during gdrive migration work.
ideas.rationale: Prevents silent data divergence between CLI and web app going undetected
ideas.key_decisions: What threshold triggers alert? Suggested >10 nodes. Where does check run — audit cron or dedicated monitor?

ideas.title: /ghost Telegram command
ideas.description: When Andreas types /ghost in Telegram, Jared detects the command, passes (1) current session key and (2) the /ghost message_id as anchor to Ghost via sessions_spawn. Ghost calls sessions_history(sessionKey, limit=N) internally to read back from the anchor — Ghost decides how much context to consume. Ghost responds as Andreas would. Jared presents as '👻 Ghost: <reply>'. Correction loop updates Ghost MEMORY.md. Trade-offs: Pro: Ghost reads raw messages (preserves tone/texture for impersonation), Ghost controls context window, no Conductor-summary bias. Con: one extra tool call inside Ghost session. Better than inline paste (fewer tokens) and better than summary (higher fidelity).
ideas.rationale: Zero-friction way to invoke Ghost mid-conversation without explaining the full context manually. Feels native to Telegram UX.
ideas.key_decisions: Default limit for sessions_history? Suggest 15-20 messages. Should Ghost read further if conversation references earlier decisions? Yes — Ghost's call. Register /ghost in channels.telegram.customCommands for native Telegram menu entry.

ideas.title: CC completion → OpenClaw system message routing
ideas.description: TESTED 2026-02-27: CC can send to any OpenClaw agent session via curl to /tools/invoke. Routing is explicit — CC specifies the target sessionKey. Both paths confirmed working: (1) agent:conductor:telegram:direct:29216737 → delivers directly to Andreas Telegram; (2) agent:main:main → Jared relays to Telegram. CC reads gateway token from openclaw.json. No MCP needed for basic routing — raw HTTP sufficient. Trade-offs: Pro: works NOW with zero infra changes. Con: gateway token in CC context = security surface; MCP would be cleaner long-term.
ideas.rationale: Unlocks CC as a first-class async subagent, not just a blocking tool call
ideas.key_decisions: RESOLVED: routing is deterministic. Open: should token be passed via env var vs file read? MCP still worth doing for cleaner interface (idea #6).

ideas.title: MCP server wrapping OpenClaw tools for CC access
ideas.description: Build a lightweight MCP server exposing sessions_send, message tool, and cron so CC can call them natively during any task. Turns CC into a full agent capable of cross-agent communication. Trade-offs: Pro: CC becomes fully autonomous agent. Con: MCP server is extra infra to maintain; /tools/invoke HTTP endpoint already exists and may be simpler.
ideas.rationale: Removes the main limitation of CC-as-subagent: no OpenClaw tool access
ideas.key_decisions: MCP vs direct /tools/invoke HTTP? Auth model for MCP? Which tools are highest priority to expose?

ideas.title: CC as general-purpose smart subagent (beyond coding)
ideas.description: Route analysis, planning, structured docs, and heavy reasoning tasks to CC instead of Sonnet API. Leverages Max 5x subscription headroom (~1080 turns/day capacity). Trade-offs: Pro: major cost reduction if Max 5x active; CC has strong reasoning, long context, file access. Con: no OpenClaw tool access natively (addressed by MCP idea); higher warm-up latency; less suited for conversational back-and-forth.
ideas.rationale: Primary cost lever if Max 5x plan is adopted — offload inference from API billing to subscription
ideas.key_decisions: Which task types route well to CC? What's the quality delta vs Sonnet for non-coding tasks? How to handle tool-dependent tasks?

ideas.title: MarkdownLM — AI coding rules as MCP infrastructure
ideas.description: Treats coding rules as infrastructure via MCP. Define once, enforced across all agents/sessions. Revisit at M1 when Forge reactivates and multi-codebase rule drift becomes real. Currently CLAUDE.md per-repo is sufficient. https://markdownlm.com

ideas.title: OpenClaw SaaS offering

ideas.key_decisions: Pricing: per-seat 9/mo; multi-tenant with data isolation

ideas.title: Atlas memory persistence via vector DB
ideas.description: Replace flat MEMORY.md with a vector store for semantic recall across sessions.
ideas.rationale: Better long-term context; enables fuzzy search over past decisions.
ideas.key_decisions: Which vector DB (Qdrant, Chroma, pgvector?); cost vs self-hosted

ideas.title: Telos mobile app
ideas.description: Native mobile app for Telos goal tracking.
ideas.rationale: Considered but web dashboard is sufficient for current use case.
ideas.key_decisions: Rejected — too much effort, web is fine
